CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Cur readers will remember that Mr. Michael Sullivan
and other ratepayers of exclusive Rathmines were
anxious to see the particulars of the enormous amount of
£9,459 13s. spent, or squandered, in ‘‘ Parliamentary
and Law Costs ’’ during 1905-6 in a vain effort to pass
a Bill that was ultimately thrown out by a majority of
210 in the House of Commons. Tt is provided by statute
that seven clear days before the commencement of the
avdit, books, vouchers, etc., shall be open to inspection.
Previous to the audit it was found that there were no.

~ vouchers for this huge sum of £9,459 13s. open for

inspection, and this, it seems clear to us, was a gross
violation of the regulations. ~Mr. P. J. O’Driscoll
formally objected in writing to this state of affairs.

One day last week a letter was received from the
. auditor, Mr. King, stating that the vouchers,
f this enormous expenditure might be inspected on
Was this a climb-down of the L.G.B.
compliance with the statutory

regulations? By whose authority were the vouche}'s. open
to inspection not that a formal protest and publicity in
the Leaper had taken place? Would the vouchers be open
to inspection for seven days? Would the fact be publicly
announced, and would the auditor take objections on
the items of this enormous expenditure of £9,459 13s.!

The editor of this paper, in his capacity as ratepayer,
went with Mr. Michael Sullivan, Mr. P. J. O’Driscoll,
and Mr. O’Rattigan to the Town Hall on Thursday last.
Mr. King, the highly-paid, and for all we know to the
contrary, highly-eflicient, L.G.B. auditor, was there.
The question that had been raised was, whether or not
the statutory regulations had been complied with. But
an L.G.B. auditor is perhaps like an eel; it is hard to
hold him when you have caught him. Mr. King would
not, or could not, give a straight answer to the straight
question, whether or not we were entitled to see the
vcuchers. By statute the vouchers should be open to
inspection, but when Mr. King was asked if we
were limited to a day he merely simpered out
that he did not wish to restrict us as to time
or deprive us of facilities. Really the unconscious in-
solence of these petty officials of Dublin Castle would be
aggravating to anyone who was not softened by the
humorous side of the whole thing. Mr. King informed
us that he had his work to do, as if the ratepayers who
had the bills to pay were mere cyphers! We suppose
Mr. King gets six or seven or eight hundred a year for
his job—and we know how these jobs are distributed—
and if the job were advertised for open competition you
would probably get plenty of men to take it on, and
carry it out for four or five pounds a week.

Mr. King stated that the accounts this time had been
prepared in a similar way as they had been prepared in
the past. So much the worse for the past. In the past
the ratepayers, probably feeling that Dublin Castle and
the men who got the jobs as auditors were not to be
fcught successfully in detail, left the whole matter to
the Fawsett’s and the Kings, and didn’t bother. Some
ratepayers bothered this time. Mr. King caved in; the
veuchers, etc., were put before us for inspection. Mr.
King was asked by whose authority we were now
allowed to see the vouchers, and he replied—‘ I am not
here to answer questions of that class.” Oh, mighty
King, despicable ratepayers! We would be indignant
only that the thing is so comic; the airs of this checking
clerk are a subject for comedy. The most gracious Mr.
King took it upon himself to let us see these vouchers
that we were.clearly convinced we had a right to sce.
If he did anything out of the way, we could appeal to
the L.G.B.! Appeal to hell against one of the devils!
Happy and serene suggestion! We ratepayers were to
inspect the vquchers as a favour at the hand of this
servant of the public. ‘T have a lot to do,”” said Mr.
King; the poor ratepayers have nothing to do but pay
Mr. Fawsett’s many salaries and perks.,, and the
lawyers’. Mr. King said that he did not assert that
there was a sufficient irregularity to warrant a new
audit ; yet these vouchers that were not produced in the
first instance were produced after protest by some rate-
payers. Ho stated that he had not asked any explana-
tion of the Urban Council for the fact that the vouchers
now produced out of the depths of his clemency
after a ratepayers’ protest had been made, had not been
produced in the first instance. One important fact was
that at the opening of the audit the vouchers were not,

_before Mr. King. Mr. King stated that the vouchers

were submitted to him last Monday, that is, Monday,
the 26th November. Would they have been submitted:
to him at all if some ratepayers had not raised the ques-
tion? We don’t know. 2

auditor, said— ‘T have a lot to do.”” Well, so has the
editor of this paper, and he could not afford more than
an hour of his time in looking over the vouchers. One
batch of vouchers was inscribed on the outside—‘¢ Rath-
mines Bill, 1905 and 1906. Paid Vouchers TI.” We
transcribed one of the items:—

' “Trish Lights Office, Dublin,
“ Inspectors’ Department,
“4¢h May, 1905.
“ DEAR SIR, 5
* ‘T have to acknowledge with thanks your letter
of yesterday’s date, enclosing cheque value two guineas
(£2 2s.), my fees for supplying to you extracts from
weather reports kept at Kingstown West and IHowth
Bailey Lighthouse Station.—Yours truly,
“C. P. R. JAMES.
“ Messrs. D. and T. Fitzgerald,
20 St. Andrew St.”

We don’t guarantee the accuracy of the “C. P. R.
James,’’ but that is the best we could make of the signa-
ture.

Now, what had the weather reports to do with the
Rathmines Bill? All we can make out of it is
that perhaps Mr. Edmundson, Mr. Fawsett, the many-
jobbed clerk, insisted on getting expert advice about the
cendition of the sea before they ventured to travel at
the ratepayers’ expense to Holyhead. Surely a doctor’s
fee for reporting on the state of Mr. Fawsett’s liver
before going to Holyhead would be quite as reasonable!
Probably there will be more about this audit in the
LEADER.

About four o’clock on Friday evening a placard of an
evening paper informed us that some men from Athenry
were on trial for cattle-driving at the Four Courts. We
take a special interest in Athenry since we struck across
those two representatives of ‘“ great ’’ London dailies on
the look-out for outrages; so we wen? to the Four Courts
and arrived whilst the jury were away deliberating over
the case. Mr. Willie Reilly was one of the nine
prisoners at the bar. He looked fresh and well, and
cracked jokes with the policemen. There were more
policemen than prisoners in the court; perhaps the
authorities were afraid that some ill-disposed person
might attempt to abduct Willie Reilly and his eight col-
leagues. The other prisoners were, all but one, decent,
kindly-looking young men, the remaining ‘‘traver-
ser”’ was an honest-looking, bearded fellow, who
answered, if we mistake not, to the name of
Michael Murphy. The light, comparatively dapper
R.I.C. men were in strong contrast to the
heavy, ponderous D.M.P.’s.  One perky policeman,
with braid on his jacket—he may have been a sergeant
or a D.I.—looked rather comical and self-conscious.
Willie Reilly was joking with him, but the law did not
appear inclined to laugh with the ‘“traverser.” ~ We
were waiting over half an hour, and were gefting tired
of looking at the bobbies and the traversers, and tired
of the back view of some of the junior bar. At last the
jury came back, and Willie Reilly yawned. The judge
—a fussy-looking man, who struck us as the sort of a
judge you might see in a comic picture, or in a musical
comedy—was quite annoyed that there was not a verdict,
and that the jury disagreed. ITe muttered something
about ““the most degrading experience;”’ and then a

A correspondent writes :—“T enclese you a conple of
newspaper cuttings from one of which you will observe
that a concerb was recently held in Kilmallock for the
purpose of raising a fund to provide coal for the poor.
With the object, of course, everyone must be in thorough
sympathy ; it is only when the programme comes to be
considered that the necessity for cism arises. The
newspaper states that ¢ Captain TFitzpatrick, R.M., won
much applause in his treatment of two comic songs,
but how his treatment of one of the comics’ came to
be received with applause in a town where a branch of
the Gaelic League exists as well as an Ix‘ish-Irelax!d
movement is rather puzzling. The worthy captain
staggered on to the platform in the get-up of a drunken
Trish sweep, and told us the funny things that happened
to Micky Cooney in connection with his elopement. One
weuld think in those enlightened days that when an
Trishman—for T believe Captain Fitzpatrick, R.M., is
one—appears before an Irish audience for the purpose
of ridiculing his countrymen, he should consider himself
lucky if he escaped unhuri instead of receiving much
applause; but such is the spirit in Kilmallock. — The
other cutting has reference to the recent proceedings of
Kilmallock Petby Sessions, at which an application was
made to this same gallant captain in his capacity as
Resident Magistrate for the use of the courthouse, which
is provided and maintained out of the county funds, for
the meetings of the Ratepayers’ Protective Association.
The application was made by a shopkeeper and ratepayer
of the town, who was ordeved to be off with himself, or
he would be removed by the police. It would be time for
the County Councils to take some steps to obtain the
centrol of those petty sessions houses which they are
compelled to provide so as that they would be in a posi-
tion to grant the use of them when required for useful
purposes by the psople whcse money erected them. Some
years ago the District Council had to hold their meetings
in a store-room in the town because they had not the
permission of the magistrates to hold it in the court-
house, although the County Surveyor was after provid-
ing chairs and erecting a barrier in it for the purpose of
the Council meeting at considerable expense. It might
bs in the recollection of your readers that this Captain
TFitzpatrick, who endeavours to ridicule his countrymen,
and who threatens to have a respectable ratepayer
appearing before him in pursuit of a legitimate and
reasonable application ejected from the courthouse, was
appointed Resident Magistrate during Mr. Wyndham’s
term of office as Chief Secretary, his only qualification
being that he is some sort of connection of that gentle-
man’s, as he is possessed neither of a knowledge of the
law nor of common sense. The appointment was the
subject of some questions in Parliament at the time.”

Several readers, in answer to our request, have kindly
sent us copies of the ‘“ Leitrim Guardian,’’ the new local
paper run by Mr. C. J. Dolan, sworn member of the
British Imperial Parliament. One of the papers, dated
Noy. 9th, has a report of a local ‘‘ Hungarian ’’ meeting
at Manorhamilton. We notice that the speech of Mr.
Dolan, member of the British Parliament, was punc-
tuated twice by a ‘‘ voice ”’ who shouted ‘“ Down with
Redmond!”” Mr. Dolan’s grievance against the Empees
—he is one himself—seems to be based rather on expe-
diency than on principle. Had Mr.”Redmond adopted
a certain course, ‘‘ no honest Nationalist,”’ according to
Mr. Dolan, ‘“ would attempt to oppose him; he weuld
rather assist him.”” And again this curious ‘‘ Hunga-
rian >’ declared :—‘ Unless the Irish members wen} to
the House of Commons to fight all parties that refuse to
do justice to Ireland, they (the Irish members) had no
business going there.”” We thought that the ‘“ Hunga-
rians’’ were against ‘‘ the floor,”” or pretended to be
against it on principle; evidently this combined ‘‘ Hun-
garian >’ and member of the British Parliament, Mr. C.
J. Dolan, is only against ‘‘ the floor ’’ as a matter of
commonplace -expediency !

Mr. Joseph O’Flaherty, T.C., Loughrea, in the course

““What were the Parliamentary Party doing at present?
They were merely copying Sinn Fein methods and hold-

Was it not cruel ““ to give the
show away ’’ like that? ‘ Faked public meetings’ are
then the meShods of the ‘ Hungarians !’

The “ Hungarians,”” we understand, are of opinion
that people—we suppose particularly other people—
should pay moré than market price if necessary for goods

In our issue of November the 9th we wrote:—‘ Mr.
Delan, the unresigned, who is an M.P., and yet is
against going to Westminster, wants to spend his Elec-
tion Fund on an industry now, we see; that might be a
convenien’ way of retreating from his impending defeat,
but do not the subscribers expect to see some feathers fly-
ing for their money. IHad Mr. Dolan made a collection
for an industry, we are sorry to have to say he would
not, in our opinion, have got such a generous response.
Such is the perversity of our childlike public. ~ People
expect to see an election ‘ scrap’ in North Leitrim, and
Mr. Dolan’s importance will dwindle into his boots, we
expect, if he puts the election fund into a laundry works
or a toy factory in Manorhamilton.” In Mr. Dolan’s
““ Leitrim Guardian’’ we see a rather different version
of his offer. Tt appears that Mr. Dolan’s proposal is
that the election as between him and his opponent should
be taken informally by the electors, and that the victor
should then go in unopposed. Mr. Dolan’s paper cal-
culates that an informal election of this kind would only
cost about £35. We have our doubts on that point, but
let it pass; an election in the usual way would cost at
least £1,000, according to the paper. Mr. Dolan pro-
poses to have an informal election, and that he should
give £500 for the establishment of an industry provided
that the Irish Party also gave £500 of their funds. We
don’t know whether the Party would feel themselves
entitled to spend £500 of publicly subscribed money that
way. But the Party might agree to the informal
election provided Mr. Dolan handed over £500 for the
establishment of some little industry rather than go
squandering it in a useless contest. But supposing the
informal election came off, and the ¢ Hungarians ’’ were
routed, and that they said they would not abide by the
decision, what then? Perhaps there would be threatened
““ proceedings,”” as in the case of the drum!

LORD DUNRAYEN AND IRISH INDUSTRY.

HE inaugural address at the Irish Industrial Con-
T ference at Limerick was delivered by Lord Dun-
raven. So far as we can judge by the address, as re-
perted, his lordship had nothing distinctive to say. He
uttered that rather shoddy sentiment that Ireland
would never become a great manufacturing country, and
h» hoped it never would. We are getting tired of the
cheap virtue of this ejaculation. Men and women of
Ireland emigrate and go into factory life in England
and America, and the pious ones at home, who have not
faced the industrial problem in Ireland, salve their con-
sciences by expressing a wish that we will never have a
big factory development here. Is not this stupidity or
hypocrisy? What would one think of the pious father
who said that he would not allow his son to mix with
thieves in Ireland and then exported him over to thieves
in England? Unfortunately, the fear that is expressed
that Ireland might become a big factory country, is
about as sensible as the fear of some soft-hearted people
who ask what would become of the poor brewers’ work-
men if we all became teetotalers; not much chance of
either state of affairs coming about. This cheap cry
about Ireland and factories might be given a rest.

Lord Dunraven truly remarked that, ‘‘a bountiful
Providence had been very beneficent to Ireland—they
did not make the most of the gifts that nature had pro-
vided them with.”” e forgot, however, or perhaps
declined,. to add that the Devil sent landlords over here
and counteracted to a great, an incalculable extent,
the bounty of Providence!

The amount of money in Ireland that ‘“ might’’ be
profitably, etc., was again trotted out. Like ‘‘the tide
of emigration,” and the ‘“no far distant date,”” that
money 1s rather stale. We will not stay to deal with
the point now; probably we will write an article on it
shortly.

Lord Dunraven declared—*‘ Nature, fortunately, had
not provided Ireland with the means of becoming a
great manufacturing country, and he did not believe
such a mission in life was suited to, or consonant with,
the mature and characteristics of the Irish people.”
Now that is the kind of thing you might expect to hear
at a juvenile debating society. Poor Nature! When
Nature put her foot down, as alleged, on industries in
Treland, she had, at least, a stout ally in English
Government! As for the Irishman’s mission in life and
manufacture, are there no great manufacturers and
industrial workers amongst the Irish in America? We
must have factories in Ireland, and it is for ourselves
and the guardians of morals to see that they be con-
ducted with a minimum amount of moral or rather im-
moral risk.

We were rather surprised to see that Lord Dunraven

talked that pernicious clap-trap about consumers paying
more for things because they are Irish. HHe said:—
*“It was the duty of the manufacturers to produce
articles as good as could be produced anywhere else, and
when this duty was done by the producer the general

STANDARDS OF COMFORT.

T has become a commonplace of criticism—so com-
monplace that we swallow without masticating, and
so don’t properly digest the truth conveyed—to -say
that we have no history of Ireland. If we had a decent
all-round history, and schools to match, we would, T
suppose, learn at school many historic facts that, as
things are, we have at great trouble to fish out for our-
selves, or else take for granted on the word of our
favourite experts in such matters writing in our
favourite contemporary periodical. = And as regards
reasoning on those facts and deducing from them rules
for our guidance politically, we would, I suppose, have
that, too, done for us by our masters at school, and so
would be spared the stormy waves of amateur advice
that roar so bewilderingly round us in the political
ocean at times like the present of political shipwreck
with no sail in sight. We would, in a word, know how
to anticipate and prepare for coming events instead of
having to wait for the events to come (and go) to
teach us how we ought to have met them.

That is one way, the popular way, of looking at the
question. There is, however, another way of looking
at it. Tor my part, ’'m not so sure that it isn’t an
advantage to us to be without this great monumental
history of our country. The logic of any particular
history, like the wisdom contained in a proverb, when
it has been made, by scholarly study of the history, so
evident that it may be set down for all to digest in
black and white in a book, is in danger, I should say,
any day thenceforward, of being packed away labelled
on a shelf in men’s minds and made no further use of.
But when men are sure of nothing in their history, and
so are under the constant necessity of arguing with each
other about it, and.when and while accordingly every
second man has a different recipe founded on a differing
historical precedent for every political trouble that turns
up, as long as that is the case, the men’s political wits
are kept on edge, and there is no chance of any man
cutting a path for himself to preeminence as a States-
man. I don’t know, for instance, but that it is because
we in Ireland are so keen on our history, no two of
us agreeing about it, that we have managed to produce so
many sharp politicians. And we are, too, when all is said,
ahead of a lot of our neighbours in the knowledge of
what ought to be done—for we haven’t ourselves the
power of doing it—as regards agrarian labour, and even
high constitutional present-day problems. If we had
our historical knowledge cut and dry for us, we would
probably allow our historical senses or faculties to rust.

This particular parish, however, though typical of
many, is not, of course, typical of all. There are many
parishes and many baronies in which emigration is a
physical cconomic necessity ; parishes and baronies in
which, though the physical standard of comfort is as
low as it well could be, yet the bulk of the people are
barely able to subsist. It is'the pinch of hunger that
primarily forces people to leave such places, and the
physical or stomach economist should be the first doctor
consulted with reference to them. But even so, even in
such poor districts, inasmuch as the people cannot shut
their ears to the tales and tidings of all sorts of
pleasures, mental as well as bodily, from the outer
world, the yearning for the mental pleasures has per-
haps as much to say to their leaving home as has the
yearning for enough and of a better sort to eat. For
after all, it is absolutely certain that one’s soul doesn’t
leave one’s body till one dies.

Now if this reasoning be correct, two conclusions
follow. One conclusion is that the lower a people’s
standard of physical comfort is, the less inclined the
people will be to be discontented with their lot, the
more they will tend to increase in numbers up to the
bare subsistence limit, and the less play mental con-
siderations will have one way or another in influencing
their economic activities. Indeed, inasmuch as the
tendency is for them to remain together at home and' to
multiply, an accompanying tendency is for them to
develop a mental standard of comfort for themselves,
and to become more and more unfitted to appreciate
mental comfort of another sort. That is to say, the
lower the standard of a people’s physical comfort is, the
less in danger that people is of being extinguished, how-
soever completely they may be eclipsed from the eyes of
the world. Ireland during the 18th century, India, I
suppose, all the time, and the East generally are
examples of this. The second conclusion is the con-
verse of the first—the higher the standard of a people’s
physical comfort is raised, the more the other standards
must be raised too, besides being made thoroughly
native—if the people are to be kept at home and
together. (This is, of course, presuming that there are
neighbouring countries with still higher standards of
comfort to attract the people, as in the case of Ireland
between America and England). In other words—

passed a resolution to the effect that the rejection of

Heo is a politician to his finger tips: no more ; 1o less.
Apparently he does not understand the Irish-Treland
movement at all. Iis tone and his argument would be
relevant to thirteen years ago, and his speeches em-
brace Republicans, Hungarians, timid M.P.’s, and
Sinn Feiners of the orthodox Leaper type: in fact,

Democratic ‘‘ IHungarian ”’ to bring them together on

Nationality.””  That was, of course, no answer. For

means, not ends. So, too, with Mr. O’Brien. He has
little in common with those sections of public opinion
mentioned above, and whose divergencies of opinion on
Irish questions he constantly employs as arguments for
his own particular case. But O’Brien joins the Con-
servative section of the ‘‘ Hungarians’’ on this: He
wants “ the co-operation of Irishmen of all classes and
creeds willing to aid in the attainment of any or all of

pleting their sales in a friendly way, are to be induced
to agree to one reform after another up to self-govern-
ment, the while Irish National politics are to be pulled
down to their level. This is a confession of failure;

failure of Mr. O’Brien’s life’s work, and loss of confi-
dence in the people’s ability to fight their own battles.
